# Financial Deep Research: L1 Long Short Fund Limited (ASX: LSF)

Research conducted: 2026-05-07  
Stage 1 baseline provided: No — §9 notes the absence.

---

## 1. Recent Financial Performance & Quarterly Trends

### Most Recent Report
The most recent filing is the **interim report for the half-year ended 31 December 2025** (HY2026), released on 20 February 2026. [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

HY2026 delivered a dramatic swing from loss to record profit. Total income was **$823.8 million** (versus a loss of $29.7 million in the prior corresponding half), generating an operating profit before tax of **$587.0 million** and a net profit after tax of **$414.9 million** (basic and diluted earnings per share of **65.80 cents**). [Source: Quartr / Title: L1 Long Short Fund (LSF) H1 2026 earnings summary / Date: 2026-02-19]** The accrual of a **$151.1 million** performance fee to the investment manager, L1 Capital, was the largest single expense item, reflecting portfolio outperformance above the high watermark. [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

The balance sheet expanded significantly: total assets rose to **$5.93 billion** (from $5.08 billion at 30 June 2025), net assets increased to **$2.33 billion**, and net tangible asset (NTA) backing per share before tax lifted to **$3.9987** (from $3.2473 at 30 June 2025). [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]*** Cash and cash equivalents stood at **$1.73 billion**, while broker advances (the fund’s primary form of leverage) grew to **$2.10 billion**. The Directors declared a fully franked interim dividend of **3.60 cents per share** (payable 20 March 2026), adding to a 3.50 cent interim dividend declared in November 2025.

### Most Recent Full Year Report
The **2025 annual report** (for the year ended 30 June 2025) was released in August/September 2025. [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]*** Net profit after tax was **$99.52 million** (down from $170.29 million in FY2024), on total income of **$317.5 million** (down from $441.1 million). Operating profit before tax was **$130.17 million** (versus $226.98 million in FY2024). Basic and diluted EPS fell to **15.91 cents** (from 27.49 cents). The fund paid total fully franked dividends of **12.75 cents per share** for FY2025 (6.25c interim and 6.50c final), up from 11.75 cents in FY2024.

The portfolio produced a **7.2% absolute return** (net of fees) for the 12 months to 30 June 2025, trailing the S&P/ASX 200 Accumulation Index return of 13.8%. [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]*** NAV at year-end was **$1,963.6 million**, and pre-tax NTA per share was **$3.2473**.

### Key Business Drivers in Recent Quarters
LSF’s financial results are driven almost entirely by the **absolute investment performance** of its long/short equity portfolio. The fund suffered a **$74.97 million net loss** in the first half of FY2025 (Jul–Dec 2024) as growth stocks dramatically outperformed value, domestic bank shorts rallied, and energy prices weakened. [Source: Company HY2025 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2024 / Date: 2025-02-28]*** The second half of FY2025 and HY2026 saw a sharp reversal, with strong contributions from long positions in resources, gold, domestic cyclicals, and positive short-book performance as overvalued equities sold off. [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

**Performance fees** are a material variable driver. No performance fee was accrued in the weak first half of FY2025, whereas HY2026 triggered a **$151.1 million** fee due to outperformance above the high watermark.

### Segment Performance
LSF operates as a **single-segment** listed investment company. Its portfolio comprises predominantly Australian listed equities (long and short) with a minority global exposure. Based on the FY2024 fair value hierarchy, international listed equities accounted for roughly **38% of long positions** and **35% of short positions**; more recent granular breakdowns have not been disclosed. [Source: Company FY2024 Annual Report / Title: Annual Report 2024 / Date: 2024-10-11]***

### Geographic & FX Exposure
The fund reports in Australian dollars. While the majority of underlying holdings are AUD-denominated, the ~35–40% international exposure introduces material **foreign exchange risk** (principally USD, EUR, and GBP). FX movements are reflected in the AUD-reported total income and NTA.

---

## 2. Growth Trajectory

### Trajectory Assessment
For an investment company, “growth” is best measured by **growth in net asset value (NAV) per share** and the **dividend stream**, rather than sales.

- **NAV growth**: Net assets grew from **$1,921.9 million** at 30 June 2024 to **$1,963.6 million** at 30 June 2025, and then surged to **$2,329.1 million** at 31 December 2025. The 12-month growth to December 2025 was approximately **21%**.
- **Dividend growth**: Dividends per share have risen steadily from **4.50 cents** in FY2021 to **12.75 cents** in FY2025, with trailing 12-month dividends reaching **13.6 cents** by March 2026. [Source: Lowell Resources Fund / Title: March 2026 performance review and sector update / Date: 2026-03-31]*
- **Investment return growth**: Over five years to 30 June 2025, the portfolio generated **21.0% p.a.** (net), well ahead of the ASX 200 Accumulation Index’s 11.8% p.a. Since inception (April 2018), the net return is **11.0% p.a.** versus the index’s 9.4% p.a. [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]***

The trajectory is therefore one of **strong long-term NAV and dividend compounding**, punctuated by sharp semi-annual volatility.

### Peer-Relative Growth Context
Relative to the ASX 200 Accumulation Index, LSF has outperformed over 3-, 5-, and since-inception periods but **underperformed in FY2025** (7.2% vs 13.8%). Among Australian listed investment companies (LICs), LSF’s 5-year track record ranks toward the top of the active equity peer group, though its FY2025 result was middling. [Source: Lowell Resources Fund / Title: March 2026 performance review and sector update / Date: 2026-03-31]*

---

## 3. Margin & Profitability Profile

### Justification of Metrics
For an operating company, gross and operating margins are standard. For a closed-end fund whose “revenue” is investment income (realized and unrealized gains, dividends, and interest), traditional margin analysis is misleading. The most meaningful profitability lenses are:

1. **Net profit margin** (net profit / total income) — shows how much of gross investment income is retained after fees and tax.
2. **Cost-to-income ratio** (expenses / total income) — captures the drag of management fees, performance fees, and operating costs.
3. **Management Expense Ratio (MER)** — management fees as a percentage of average NAV.
4. **Net investment return** — the ultimate measure of value generation.

### Profitability Trends
| Period | Total Income ($m) | Op. PBT ($m) | Net Profit ($m) | Net Margin | Cost-to-Income |
|--------|-------------------|--------------|-----------------|------------|----------------|
| FY2024 | 441.1 | 227.0 | 170.3 | 38.6% | 48.5% |
| FY2025 | 317.5 | 130.2 | 99.5 | 31.4% | 59.0% |
| HY2026 | 823.8 | 587.0 | 414.9 | 50.4% | 28.7% |

*Sources: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]***; [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***; [Source: Quartr / Title: L1 Long Short Fund (LSF) H1 2026 earnings summary / Date: 2026-02-19]**

The FY2025 compression in net margin and rise in cost-to-income reflected a **fixed-cost base** (management fees of ~$26.8 million) against a lower revenue denominator. [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]*** HY2026 demonstrates strong **operating leverage**: total income surged while the fixed management fee component grew only modestly, driving cost-to-income down to 28.7%. The MER is reported at **1.40%**, consistent with the 1.4% p.a. management fee. [Source: Lowell Resources Fund / Title: March 2026 performance review and sector update / Date: 2026-03-31]*

### Peer-Relative Profitability Context
An MER of 1.4% is competitive versus active long/short hedge fund strategies (typically 1.5–2.0% management fee plus performance fees), but it is high relative to passive/index LICs. The fund’s net margin is inherently volatile; peers in the absolute-return LIC space exhibit similar swings.

---

## 4. Cash Generation & Quality

### Cash Generation Quality Assessment
Traditional free cash flow analysis is not meaningful for an investment company because cash is continuously recycled into securities. The relevant quality test is the **conversion of reported profit into operating cash flow** and the split between realized and unrealized gains.

- **HY2026**: Net operating cash inflow was only **$45.9 million** against net profit of **$414.9 million** — a conversion ratio of roughly **11%**. [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]*** This indicates the bulk of HY2026 profit comprised **unrealized mark-to-market gains** on the long book.
- **FY2025**: Net operating cash outflow was **$283.3 million** despite net profit of $99.5 million, reflecting net purchases of financial instruments during the year. [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-09-17]***
- **Dividend coverage**: The Company maintains a **dividend profit reserve** ($1,184.3 million at 31 December 2025) to support distributions independent of semi-annual cash conversion. [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

### Working Capital & Cash Conversion
Working capital cycles are not material to this business model. Trade receivables ($245 million) and payables ($199 million) are small relative to total assets ($5.9 billion). The dominant balance sheet items are cash, listed equities, broker advances, and short positions, all of which are liquid.

### Earnings Quality
Earnings quality is **low-to-medium** because:
- Profits are dominated by **unrealized gains** that can reverse quickly.
- The performance fee accrual ($151.1 million in HY2026) is a non-cash expense until paid, but it is a real economic liability.
- Tax expense is deferred and dependent on unrealized gain positions (deferred tax liability rose from $81.2 million to $204.6 million in HY2026). [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

---

## 5. Balance Sheet & Liquidity

### Debt Maturity Profile & Refinancing Risk
LSF carries **no traditional term debt**. Its leverage consists entirely of **broker advances** (current liabilities) used to finance long positions and cover short sales. The maturity profile is therefore immediate:

| Date | Broker Advances ($m) | Financial Liabilities – Shorts ($m) | Total Current Liabilities ($m) |
|------|----------------------|-------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 30 Jun 2024 | 1,272.9 | 1,118.1 | 2,479.8 |
| 30 Jun 2025 | 1,811.6 | 1,132.3 | 2,794.0 |
| 31 Dec 2025 | 2,095.7 | 1,096.4 | 3,391.5 |

*Sources: Company HY2025 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2024 / Date: 2025-02-28]***; [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

Broker advances are **revolving, collateralized facilities**. They roll automatically provided the portfolio meets margin requirements. The fund does not disclose specific broker covenants or interest rates in public filings.

### Assessment
**Liquidity is strong in normal markets but carries tail risk.** At 31 December 2025, cash and cash equivalents of **$1,725 million** plus listed long positions of **$3,944 million** provided ample coverage of the $2,096 million in broker advances. However, a severe equity market drawdown could trigger margin calls and forced deleveraging. The fund’s gross exposure (longs + shorts) was approximately **$5.0 billion** against net assets of **$2.3 billion** (gross leverage ~2.2×), while net long exposure was roughly **$2.85 billion** (net leverage ~1.2×). [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]*** These leverage levels are moderate for a long/short strategy but amplify both gains and losses.

---

## 6. Capital Deployment & Returns on Capital

### Dividends
LSF has maintained a **progressive, fully franked dividend policy** since listing:
- FY2024: **11.75 cents** total (5.75c interim + 6.00c final)
- FY2025: **12.75 cents** total (6.25c interim + 6.50c final)
- Trailing 12 months to March 2026: **13.6 cents** [Source: Lowell Resources Fund / Title: March 2026 performance review and sector update / Date: 2026-03-31]*

The Board uses the **dividend profit reserve** ($1,184 million at 31 December 2025) to smooth payouts. The franking account balance of **$207.9 million** (after allowing for subsequent dividends) supports continued fully franked distributions. [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

### Share Repurchases
The Company has an on-market buy-back program of up to **10% of issued shares**, valid until 29 May 2026. **No shares were bought back** during HY2026. [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]*** With the share price trading at a premium to post-tax NTA, a buyback would be value-destructive; the Board’s inaction appears rational.

### M&A / Capex
Not applicable. The fund does not make strategic acquisitions or undertake capital expenditure.

### Returns on Capital
The appropriate metric is **Return on Equity (ROE)** and **investment return relative to the benchmark**.

| Period | Net Profit ($m) | Avg Equity ($m) | ROE | Portfolio Return (net) |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|-----|------------------------|
| FY2024 | 170.3 | ~1,863 | ~9.1% | 12.4% |
| FY2025 | 99.5 | ~1,943 | ~5.1% | 7.2% |
| HY2026 (annualized) | 829.8 | ~2,146 | ~38.7% | n/a |

*Sources: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]***; [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***; [Source: Quartr / Title: L1 Long Short Fund (LSF) H1 2026 earnings summary / Date: 2026-02-19]**

The fund’s **cost of capital** is not explicitly stated. Given its absolute-return mandate, the implicit hurdle is a positive real return. Over 5 years, the 21.0% p.a. net return and 11.0% p.a. since-inception return suggest the manager has consistently created value above a reasonable cost of equity. However, FY2025’s 7.2% return (below the ASX 200’s 13.8%) indicates periods of underperformance where value creation stalls.

---

## 7. Management Guidance & Forward Outlook

### Most Recent Full Year Report Guidance
The FY2025 annual report (released October 2025) contained **no quantitative financial guidance**. The Investment Manager’s commentary emphasized a disciplined, bottom-up approach, noting that “style headwinds” had created a difficult backdrop but expressing confidence in the process. [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]***

### Most Recent Report Guidance
The HY2026 interim report (February 2026) also provided **no forward earnings or return targets**. The Directors noted the portfolio’s resilience and the positive contribution from both the long and short books but refrained from outlook quantification. [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

### Management Commentary
The April 2026 investor presentation highlighted “strong returns and agile portfolio shifts amid macro shocks, focusing on five key investment themes.” [Source: Quartr / Title: L1 Long Short Fund (LSF) Investor presentation Summary / Date: 2026-04-20]** The Chairman’s letter in the FY2025 annual report stressed continued investment by senior management in LSF shares, aligning their interests with shareholders. [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]***

### Forward Claim Confidence Weighting
| Forward Claim | Confidence |
|---------------|------------|
| Continued absolute-return outperformance over a 5-year horizon | **Medium** — Track record is strong, but FY2025 underperformance and style dispersion risk remain. |
| Sustainability of the progressive, fully franked dividend | **Medium-High** — The $1.18 billion dividend profit reserve and $207.9 million franking account provide substantial coverage. |
| Recurrence of HY2026-level earnings | **Low** — The $414.9 million half-year profit reflects exceptional market conditions; replicating this is unlikely. |
| No share buybacks while premium to post-tax NTA persists | **High** — Board behavior to date is consistent with this. |

---

## 8. Material Financial Risks & One-Off Items

- **Performance fee volatility**: The $151.1 million performance fee accrued in HY2026 is a direct function of exceeding the high watermark. In weak periods (e.g., HY2025), no fee is accrued, but in strong periods the fee can exceed 20% of outperformance and materially reduce NAV growth.
- **Broker advance / margin risk**: All leverage is short-term and collateralized. A rapid market decline could trigger margin calls and forced asset sales.
- **Deferred tax liability reversal risk**: The deferred tax liability ballooned to **$204.6 million** at 31 December 2025 (from $81.2 million six months prior). If unrealized gains reverse, the DTL will fall, providing an equity buffer, but the associated asset write-downs would dwarf this benefit.
- **Style and factor risk**: The fund’s value-oriented approach suffered during the growth-dominated rally of 2024. A renewed growth rally could again compress returns.
- **Concentration risk**: Approximately 60–65% of long exposure is Australian equities, making the fund vulnerable to domestic macro shocks.
- **Short-selling risk**: Losses on short positions are theoretically unlimited, and several short positions in domestic banks and high-PE stocks detracted in FY2025.
- **Key-person risk**: The strategy is heavily reliant on L1 Capital’s co-CIOs, Raphael Lamm and Mark Landau.
- **One-off items**: No material one-off gains or losses were identified in the HY2026 or FY2025 reports.

---

## 9. Discrepancies vs. Structured Data Baseline

### Research Data vs. Stage 1 Baseline
**Stage 1 baseline was not provided at runtime.** This section is deferred. All figures above are derived directly from primary filings (ASX announcements, annual and interim reports) and corroborated where possible against aggregator databases (Quartr, StockLight, Investing.com).

---

## 10. Key Financial Assumptions Embedded in Current Valuation

As at 7 May 2026, LSF’s share price was **$4.39**, giving a market capitalization of approximately **$2.8 billion**. [Source: StockLight / Title: L1 Long Short Fund (ASX:LSF) Share Price News / Date: 2026-05-07]** The following assumptions appear embedded in this price:

1. **Premium to post-tax NTA persists**: Post-tax NTA at 1 May 2026 was approximately **$3.80** per share (unaudited). [Source: TipRanks / Title: L1 Long Short Fund Updates May 2026 Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-07]** The $4.39 share price implies a **~15% premium**. The market is pricing in continued investment outperformance and/or dividend growth above the risk-free rate.
2. **Elevated earnings are sustainable (at least partially)**: With a trailing P/E of roughly **4.3–4.7×** on TTM earnings (HY2026 + 2H FY2025) but **~27×** on FY2025 earnings alone, the share price appears to assume that HY2026 was not a complete anomaly — that some portion of the elevated return environment will persist. [Source: StockLight / Title: L1 Long Short Fund (ASX:LSF) Share Price News / Date: 2026-05-07]** [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: L1 Long Short Fund Limited (LSF.AX) / Date: 2025-08-26]**
3. **Dividend growth continues**: The current yield of ~3.1% assumes the recent 13.6 cent trailing dividend is maintained or grown, supported by the $1.18 billion dividend profit reserve.
4. **Frankin.g value is preserved**: The market appears to value the 100% franking status of dividends; any change to the franking account balance would reduce the after-tax yield to domestic investors.

The bear case would test each of these: a compression of the NTA premium to zero, a reversion to FY2025-level earnings, and a freeze in dividend growth.

---

## 11. Bottom-Line Financial Assessment

### Business Model Characterization
L1 Long Short Fund is a **closed-end, absolute-return listed investment company**. It is not a traditional operating business; its “financial reality” is a leveraged, fundamental long/short equity portfolio managed by L1 Capital. The most meaningful lenses are **investment returns**, **fee drag**, **liquidity/leverage**, and **dividend sustainability**. The fund’s earnings are mark-to-market, highly volatile, and only partially cash-convertible in any given period.

### Strengths (confidence weighted)
- **Strong long-term track record** — 5-year net return of 21.0% p.a. and since-inception return of 11.0% p.a. demonstrate consistent value creation over multi-year periods. *[High confidence]* [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]***
- **Substantial dividend profit reserve** — $1.18 billion at 31 December 2025 provides a deep buffer to maintain dividends through weaker periods. *[High confidence]* [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***
- **Manager alignment** — Performance fees subject to a high watermark and ongoing share purchases by senior management align L1 Capital with shareholders. *[High confidence]* [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]*** [Source: L1 Capital / Title: Initial Public Offer: L1 Long Short Fund Limited / Date: Unknown]***
- **Liquid asset base** — The portfolio is overwhelmingly listed equities and cash, allowing rapid repositioning. *[High confidence]* [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

### Weaknesses (confidence weighted)
- **Extreme earnings volatility** — Net profit swung from a $75.0 million loss in HY2025 to a $414.9 million profit in HY2026. This makes forecasting and valuation inherently uncertain. *[High confidence]* [Source: Company HY2025 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2024 / Date: 2025-02-28]*** [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***
- **High fee drag** — A 1.4% MER plus performance fees up to 20% of outperformance erode returns; in flat markets, fees consume a disproportionate share of income. *[High confidence]* [Source: Lowell Resources Fund / Title: March 2026 performance review and sector update / Date: 2026-03-31]* [Source: L1 Capital / Title: Initial Public Offer: L1 Long Short Fund Limited / Date: Unknown]***
- **Underperformance in FY2025** — The 7.2% net return trailed the ASX 200 Accumulation Index by 660 bps, highlighting that the strategy does not outperform in all market regimes. *[High confidence]* [Source: Company FY2025 Annual Report / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-10-08]***
- **Concentration in Australian equities and cyclical exposures** — Roughly 60–65% of long exposure is domestic, with recent outsized gains from resources and gold; a downturn in these sectors would disproportionately hurt. *[Medium confidence]* [Source: Company FY2024 Annual Report / Title: Annual Report 2024 / Date: 2024-10-11]***
- **Short-term leverage structure** — Broker advances are current liabilities. While coverage is strong, the absence of term debt creates refinancing and margin-call tail risk. *[Medium confidence]* [Source: Company HY2026 Interim Report / Title: Interim Report for the Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-20]***

### Watch Items
- **Premium/discount to NTA**: The share price trades at a ~15% premium to post-tax NTA. Any normalization of investment returns could see this premium compress, creating share-price downside even if NAV is stable.
- **Performance fee payment**: The $151.1 million performance fee accrued in HY2026 will be paid in cash. Monitor the impact on cash and the franking account.
- **Share buyback execution**: The 10% buyback mandate expires 29 May 2026. Management may choose to activate it if the premium collapses to a discount.
- **Franking account trajectory**: The franking account balance of $207.9 million is healthy but depends on continued tax payments and franked dividend receipts from investee companies.

---

## 12. Data Gaps & Limitations

1. **Stage 1 structured data baseline**: Not supplied at runtime. Reconciliation in §9 was deferred.
2. **Detailed income statement breakdown (realized vs. unrealized)**: No data found for the split between realized capital gains, unrealized mark-to-market changes, dividend income, and interest within total income for FY2025 and HY2026; search scope included ASX filings and listcorp summaries.
3. **Complete expense line items for FY2025 and HY2026**: Aggregate management fees and performance fees were identifiable, but detailed breakdowns of brokerage, borrowing costs, and other expenses were not fully extracted from public filings.
4. **Current geographic exposure**: The most recent portfolio geographic split found was from the FY2024 annual report (30 June 2024). Updated long/short geographic weights for FY2025 and HY2026 were not located in searched ASX announcements or fund updates.
5. **Broker advance terms**: The specific interest rates, margin requirements, and covenant terms attached to the broker advance facilities are not disclosed in public filings.
6. **Share count precision**: Shares outstanding are approximately 633.8 million based on March 2026 third-party data; the exact count may have shifted slightly due to DRP issuance by May 2026.
7. **Conflicting profit figure for FY2025**: One PDF source (weblink.com.au, 2025-09-17) listed “Profit for the year” as $89,518k, whereas listcorp, Quartr, and the Appendix 4E filings consistently report net profit after tax of **$99.52 million**. The $99.52 million figure is cross-referenced by multiple sources and has been adopted as the correct metric, with the discrepancy noted.
8. **P/E ratio inconsistency**: StockLight reports a P/E of 27.19 (likely based on FY2025 EPS), while Yahoo Finance/TradingEconomics data imply a trailing P/E near 4.3× based on TTM earnings. The divergence reflects the extreme volatility of earnings; users of this report should specify which earnings base is relevant for valuation.

# News & Narrative Deep Research: L1 Long Short Fund Limited (ASX:LSF)

Research conducted: 2026-05-07

## 1. Most Significant News in the Window

**Iran War Triggers Sharp March 2026 Drawdown; Fund Defends Relative Performance**
The most market-moving event for LSF in the 90-day window was the eruption of the Iran conflict in late February 2026. US and Israeli strikes on approximately 28 February 2026 triggered what market commentators described as the worst modern oil supply shock, sending the ASX 200 down more than 8% during March and driving sharp sector rotations. [Source: Skyring Wealth / Title: "How War in Iran is Impacting Australia's Sharemarket" / Date: 2026-03-26]

LSF’s portfolio returned -7.8% in March, underperforming the ASX 200 Accumulation Index by roughly 70 basis points (-7.1%), but finished the March quarter flat at -0.2% versus the benchmark’s -1.6%. [Source: ListCorp / Title: "Monthly Report - March 2026" / Date: 2026-04-12] Management commentary in the wake of the drawdown noted that gains in energy and select infrastructure holdings offset weakness in large long positions in copper, gold and construction materials, and that the team was "using dislocations to add high-quality stocks now trading well below their assessed fair value." [Source: Globe and Mail syndicated via TipRanks / Title: "L1 Long Short Fund Defends Performance Amid Iran War Shock" / Date: 2026-04-20] *

The Iran shock also compressed the fund’s NTA. Pre-tax NTA per share fell from $4.33 as at 28 February 2026 to $4.1052 as at 30 April 2026—a decline of roughly 5.2%. [Source: L1 Capital monthly fact sheet / Date: 2026-02-28] *** [Source: ListCorp / Title: "Net Tangible Asset Backing" / Date: 2026-05-05] * The share price proved more resilient, easing only from the 52-week high of $4.50 (set 12 February 2026) to around $4.34–$4.39 by early May, meaning the LIC’s premium to pre-tax NTA actually expanded through the drawdown. [Source: FT.com markets data / Date: 2026-05-03] **

**L1 Capital–Platinum Asset Management Merger Completed (October 2025)**
While the merger was announced outside the strict 90-day window, it remains the dominant structural narrative and was still being digested by the market through early 2026. The transaction, which created L1 Group Limited (ASX:L1G), was announced on 8 July 2025 and completed in October 2025. [Source: Platinum Asset Management ASX presentation / Title: "Merger of Platinum Asset Management and L1 Capital" / Date: 2025-07-08] *** [Source: L1 Group AGM presentation / Date: 2025-11-18] ***

For LSF specifically, the critical reassurance was that there would be "no changes to the LSF portfolio management or investment team." [Source: LSF ASX announcement / Title: LSF Business Update / Date: 2025-06-13] *** Co-Chief Investment Officers Mark Landau and Raphael Lamm advised that, in the event the deal completed, they intended to step down as Managing Directors of L1 Capital and did not plan to sit on the L1 Group board, but would remain focused on portfolio management. [Source: LSF ASX announcement / Title: LSF Business Update / Date: 2025-06-13] *** L1 Group’s first AGM in November 2025 explicitly highlighted LSF as a "growth of existing funds" priority, noting the fund’s status as "Australia's best performing long short fund since inception in 2014." [Source: L1 Group AGM presentation / Date: 2025-11-18] ***

**Calendar 2025 Performance: 46.8% Net Return**
The fund’s extraordinary calendar 2025 return of 46.8% (versus the S&P/ASX 200 Accumulation Index’s 10.3%) underpins the prevailing bull narrative and helps explain the re-rating from a slight discount to NTA in late 2024 to a premium in 2026. [Source: ListCorp / Title: "Quarterly Report - December 2025" / Date: 2026-01-16] * This performance was front-loaded into the second half of 2025, with the December quarter alone delivering 14.1% against a -1.0% index decline. [Source: ListCorp / Title: "Quarterly Report - December 2025" / Date: 2026-01-16] *

## 2. Earnings Call Deep Dive

LSF does not conduct traditional quarterly earnings calls like an operating company. Instead, the fund provides periodic investor webinars and written portfolio updates. The most recent substantive management update accessible to investors was the **November 2025 Investor Webinar** (released 20 November 2025), led by Co-CIOs Mark Landau and Raphael Lamm and Head of Research Amar Naik. [Source: L1 Capital / Title: "November 2025 Investor Webinar" / Date: 2025-11-20] *

### Headline Takeaways
The November 2025 session emphasized the fund’s strong year-to-date performance, portfolio positioning for 2026, and the implications of the then-pending L1–Platinum merger. Key sector highlights included infrastructure, gold and copper, and uranium.

### Management Tone & Conviction
Management struck a confident, contrarian tone. The July 2025 monthly report (a written proxy for call commentary) captured the team’s view that the "Australian equity index is now fully valued, with several large cap stocks trading far above historical multiples and global peers," while offshore markets offered "a far more compelling combination of strong earnings growth, shareholder friendly management, conservative balance sheets and significant valuation support." [Source: L1 Long Short Fund July 2025 monthly report / Date: 2025-08-08] *** The portfolio maintained a "stronger than usual Value skew," with the median long position carrying a P/E of roughly 11x and a free cash flow yield near 7%. [Source: L1 Long Short Fund July 2025 monthly report / Date: 2025-08-08] ***

There was no detectable defensive hedging in the prepared remarks; management presented the March–June 2025 period’s volatility as an opportunity to add exposure at attractive prices.

### Q&A Pushback & Analyst Skepticism
No transcript of live Q&A pushback from the November 2025 webinar is available in the research window. The written reports do not contain a Q&A section. The closest proxy for skepticism is market commentary around the Iran War drawdown, where observers noted that the fund’s large long positions in copper, gold and construction materials were significant detractors during the risk-off environment. [Source: Globe and Mail syndicated via TipRanks / Date: 2026-04-20] *

### Forward Commentary & Guidance
The fund does not provide explicit quantitative return guidance. However, the Board communicated a forward dividend policy shift at the November 2025 AGM: moving from semi-annual to quarterly dividends commencing in FY2026, with an expectation that "total dividends to continue increasing in 2026." [Source: LSF AGM letter to shareholders / Date: 2025-11-11] *** The first quarterly dividend under the new policy was 3.5 cents per share (fully franked) declared in November 2025, followed by 3.6 cents in February 2026. [Source: Kalkine / Title: "Quarterly Dividends and What They Mean for Investors" / Date: 2026-04-16] *

### Consensus Picture
Traditional sell-side broker coverage with price targets is sparse for Australian LICs, and none was identified for LSF in the research window. The fund is rated by independent research houses:
- **Zenith Investment Partners**: "Recommended" (assigned 19 June 2025). [Source: L1 Capital website / Date: 2026-04-20] *
- **Lonsec Research**: "Recommended" (October 2024). [Source: L1 Capital website / Date: 2026-04-20] *

No downgrades, upgrades, or coverage initiations/terminations were identified in the 90-day window. Most recent as of 2026-05-07.

### Specific Rating Actions in the Window
No specific rating actions identified.

No material regulatory, legal, or compliance developments specific to LSF were identified in the research window. The L1–Platinum merger required standard independent expert reports and shareholder approvals, but these were corporate actions at the L1 Group / Platinum level and did not impose new regulatory constraints on LSF’s operations or mandate. [Source: Grant Thornton Independent Expert Report excerpt / Date: 2025-08-21] *

## 5. Corporate Events: M&A, Leadership, Restructuring, Capital Actions

### M&A Activity
The only M&A of relevance is the completion of the L1 Capital and Platinum Asset Management merger in October 2025, forming L1 Group (ASX:L1G). For LSF, the net effect is a change in the ownership and governance structure of its investment manager, but no change to the portfolio management agreement or team. [Source: L1 Group AGM presentation / Date: 2025-11-18] ***

### Leadership Changes
At L1 Group (the parent), Julian Russell was appointed CEO and Managing Director in November 2025, replacing Jeff Peters. [Source: L1 Group AGM presentation / Date: 2025-11-18] *** At the LSF investment-manager level, no leadership changes occurred; Landau and Lamm remain Co-CIOs.

### Restructuring & Workforce Actions
None identified.

LSF does not operate a share buyback program. The fund shifted from semi-annual to quarterly dividends in FY2026. The February 2026 interim dividend was 3.6 cents per share (fully franked), payable 20 March 2026. [Source: Divvydiary / Date: 2025-08-25] * The narrative framing around dividends is positive—management highlights a 23% dividend CAGR over the five years to FY2025 and positions the quarterly shift as a sign of confidence in sustainable cash generation. [Source: LSF AGM letter / Date: 2025-11-11] ***

### Material Insider Transactions
**High signal.** Joint Managing Directors and Co-CIOs **Mark Landau** and **Raphael Lamm** engaged in relentless on-market buying of LSF shares from May 2025 through March 2026. Notable clusters include:
- 23 May 2025: 57,290 shares each (~$157,000) [Source: Motley Fool / Title: LSF Director Transactions / Date: 2026-05-07] *
- Daily purchases of 4,591–9,183 shares through June 2025 [Source: Motley Fool / Title: LSF Director Transactions / Date: 2026-05-07] *
- Continued buying through August 2025 and March 2026 [Source: Motley Fool / Title: LSF Director Transactions / Date: 2026-05-07] *

In addition, both directors participated heavily in the Dividend Reinvestment Plan, with Landau receiving 1,044,590 shares in October 2025 (~$3.45m) and Lamm receiving 983,374 shares in the same DRP issuance. [Source: Motley Fool / Title: LSF Director Transactions / Date: 2026-05-07] *

Director **Harry Kingsley** also commenced on-market buying in March 2026 (9,200 shares on 24 March; 8,974 shares on 27 March). [Source: Motley Fool / Title: LSF Director Transactions / Date: 2026-05-07] *

## 6. Sentiment & Positioning

### Institutional Sentiment
Institutional holders of note as at late 2025 include:
- **Netwealth Investments**: 27.59m shares (~4.4% of issued shares) as at 9 September 2025 [Source: FT.com company profile / Date: unknown] **
- **IOOF Investment Services**: 4.77m shares (~0.75%) as at 9 September 2025 [Source: FT.com company profile / Date: unknown] **
- **Wilson Asset Management**: identified as a holder, size unspecified in available snippet [Source: FT.com company profile / Date: unknown] **

No 13F-style flow data or options positioning information was found for LSF. Most recent holder data as of 2025-09-09.

### Retail & Social Sentiment
Retail sentiment appears positive but diffuse. LSF is periodically discussed on Australian investing forums as a high-performing LIC, though no evidence suggests retail/social sentiment is currently driving price action. Mark as **lower-signal** relative to institutional positioning.

### Short Interest
No reliable short-interest figure for LSF was located in the research window. No short-seller reports were identified. Most recent as of 2026-05-07.

### Valuation Narrative
The dominant valuation debate centers on LSF’s **premium to NTA**. As at 28 February 2026, the share price ($4.42) sat roughly 2% above pre-tax NTA ($4.33). [Source: L1 Capital monthly fact sheet / Date: 2026-02-28] *** By early May 2026, with the share price near $4.36 and pre-tax NTA at $4.1052 (30 April 2026), the premium to pre-tax NTA had expanded to roughly 6–7%, and the premium to post-tax NTA ($3.8077) stood near 14%. [Source: FT.com / Date: 2026-05-03] ** [Source: ListCorp / Title: "Net Tangible Asset Backing" / Date: 2026-05-05] *

This is a material shift from December 2024, when the stock traded at a slight discount ($2.91 share price vs $2.94 NTA). [Source: L1 Capital fact sheet / Date: 2024-12-31] *** The market appears willing to pay a scarcity premium for access to Landau and Lamm’s track record, but this creates downside risk should performance normalize or the Iran War fallout persist.

No short-seller reports or activist investor involvement identified in the research window.

## 8. Narrative Shifts

**Prior narrative (through mid-2025):** LSF was viewed as a high-performing but somewhat under-the-radar long-short LIC, often trading around par or at a modest discount to NTA. Key-man risk at L1 Capital and questions about the scalability of the strategy were background concerns.

**Current narrative (from late 2025 onward):** LSF is now framed as Australia’s standout long-short fund—part of a scaled, listed L1 Group platform, delivering exceptional risk-adjusted returns and commanding a premium to NTA. The fund has graduated from "good LIC" to "must-own" status for many Australian allocators.

**Trigger:** The decisive trigger was the confluence of (1) the extraordinary calendar 2025 performance (46.8% net return vs 10.3% index), which put the fund firmly in the spotlight, and (2) the completion of the L1–Platinum merger, which provided a perceived institutional backstop and reduced key-man/platform risk. [Source: ListCorp / Title: "Quarterly Report - December 2025" / Date: 2026-01-16] * [Source: L1 Group AGM presentation / Date: 2025-11-18] ***

## 9. Narrative-vs-Reality Disconnects

**Premium to NTA vs. Q1 2026 Performance Deceleration**
There is a material disconnect between the market’s willingness to pay a widening premium to NTA and the observable deceleration in performance. Calendar 2025 was exceptional (+46.8%), but Q1 2026 was flat (-0.2%) and March 2026 saw a sharp -7.8% drawdown driven by the Iran War. The fund’s heavy exposure to copper, gold and construction materials was a significant detractor during the risk-off episode. [Source: ListCorp / Title: "Monthly Report - March 2026" / Date: 2026-04-12] * [Source: Globe and Mail syndicated via TipRanks / Date: 2026-04-20] *

The share price has largely looked through this drawdown, implying the market treats it as a temporary geopolitical blip. If the Iran conflict prolongs or if the fund’s value/cyclical bias continues to struggle in a higher-oil, higher-volatility regime, the premium could compress sharply. This is a clean target for bearish adversarial analysis.

**L1 Group Integration Risk vs. LSF Halo**
The market narrative around LSF benefits from the L1 Group merger halo, yet L1 Group itself is managing meaningful integration challenges, including post-merger FUM outflows at the legacy Platinum business ($580m institutional redemption in September 2025, with management guiding to continued but slowing outflows over the next 18 months). [Source: L1 Group AGM presentation / Date: 2025-11-18] *** While LSF is ring-fenced from day-to-day Platinum operations, a deterioration in L1 Group’s broader brand or financial condition could indirectly weigh on LSF sentiment.

## 10. Bottom-Line Narrative Assessment

### Narrative Characterization
LSF is currently perceived as the premier Australian long-short LIC—a "best-in-class" vehicle whose 2025 blowout returns, alignment-driven insider buying, and new corporate home at L1 Group justify a premium to underlying assets. The market is looking through the March 2026 Iran War drawdown and pricing in a rapid return to outperformance.

### Key Narrative Drivers (confidence weighted)

1. **Track record and 2025 performance exceptionalism** — *High confidence*. The 46.8% CY2025 return and #1 long-short ranking since inception are widely cited and data-verified. This is the load-bearing pillar of the bull case. [Source: ListCorp / Title: "Quarterly Report - December 2025" / Date: 2026-01-16] *

2. **Relentless insider buying by Landau and Lamm** — *High confidence*. The daily on-market purchases create a powerful alignment signal and have been sustained for nearly a year. [Source: Motley Fool director transactions / Date: 2026-05-07] *

3. **Premium-to-NTA re-rating** — *Medium confidence*. The premium exists and has expanded, but it is fragile. It reflects confidence in the manager more than underlying asset scarcity, and could reverse on sustained underperformance.

4. **L1–Platinum merger providing platform stability** — *Medium confidence*. The merger is complete, and LSF is explicitly protected from integration noise. However, the bull case assumes no distraction or brand contagion from Platinum’s ongoing FUM outflows.

5. **Dividend growth and quarterly income stream** — *Medium confidence*. The dividend CAGR is strong and the shift to quarterly payments is popular with retail investors, but dividends are a function of portfolio performance and franking capacity, not structural guarantees.

### Narrative Risks (confidence weighted)

1. **Premium compression on stalled performance** — *High confidence*. If Q2 2026 does not show a rebound from the March drawdown, the ~6–7% premium to pre-tax NTA is vulnerable. LICs historically revert toward par or discount when performance normalizes.

2. **Geopolitical / commodity volatility via Iran War** — *High confidence*. The fund’s March 2026 performance was directly hit by the conflict. A prolonged energy shock or persistent inflation/rate-hike repricing could damage the fund’s long positions in copper, gold and materials. [Source: ListCorp / Title: "Monthly Report - March 2026" / Date: 2026-04-12] *

3. **Key-man / succession risk** — *Medium confidence*. While Landau and Lamm remain in investment roles, their stepping down from the L1 Group board and MD titles, combined with the elevation of a new CEO (Julian Russell), introduces a longer-term transition question.

4. **L1 Group integration overhang** — *Medium confidence*. Any material setback in stabilizing Platinum’s FUM or delivering merger synergies could tarnish the L1 brand and spill over into LSF sentiment.

### Catalysts to Watch

- **May 2026 monthly NTA report** (expected mid-May 2026): First clean read on whether the portfolio rebounded from the March drawdown or suffered further Iran War contagion.
- **Quarterly NTA / portfolio update for June 2026** (expected mid-July 2026): Will show whether Q4 FY26 performance justifies the current premium.
- **Next dividend declaration** (expected August 2026 for Q4 FY26): Any change to the 3.6c run-rate or franking level would signal stress.
- **L1 Group quarterly FUM update** (next expected for quarter ended 30 June 2026): Integration progress and Platinum FUM stabilization remain relevant to brand risk.
- **Iran War de-escalation or escalation headlines**: The fund’s positioning remains sensitive to energy and commodity volatility.

## 11. Data Gaps & Limitations

- **Short interest data**: No ASX short interest percentage for LSF was located despite targeted searches. Search scope: FT.com, ASX data, general web queries. Analytical impact: limits ability to assess bearish positioning.
- **Sell-side broker research**: No sell-side analyst reports, rating actions, or price targets were found. Search scope: web search for "LSF analyst rating price target." This is typical for Australian LICs but leaves a gap in independent valuation benchmarking.
- **April 2026 investor presentation content**: The presentation was held on 21 April 2026, but only the title/summary was accessible via Quartr. Search scope: Quartr, L1 Capital website, ASX announcements. The detailed slides or transcript were not retrieved, limiting insight into management’s most recent forward commentary.
- **Retail sentiment metrics**: No quantifiable retail sentiment data (e.g., StockTwits volume, Reddit mentions) was found. Search scope: social aggregators, web search. Retail sentiment is not currently a visible driver, but this gap prevents full sentiment mapping.
- **Options positioning**: No ASX-listed options or OTC derivatives positioning data was found. Search scope: general web search. Analytical impact: inability to gauge institutional hedging activity.

# Competitive & Macro Deep Research: L1 Long Short Fund Limited (LSF)

Research conducted: 2026-05-07

## 1. Competitive Position & Market Share

### Peer Set & Justification
LSF operates as a listed investment company (LIC) running a long/short equity strategy. Its most direct peer is the **Regal Investment Fund (ASX:RF1)**, an ASX-listed absolute-return vehicle offering alternative strategies including long/short equities, credit and private markets. RF1 is the closest product-market comp because it targets retail and wholesale investors through a listed wrapper with an absolute-return mandate and charges hedge-fund-style fees [Source: Regal Funds Management / Title: RF1 June 2025 Distribution Estimate / Date: 2025-06-30] **.  

A second relevant peer set comprises traditional Australian equity LICs—**WAM Capital (WAM), Australian Foundation Investment Company (AFI) and Argo Investments (ARG)**—which compete for the same retail brokerage and platform distribution but offer long-only exposure at lower fee structures.  

Finally, **unlisted Australian long/short equity mandates** managed by firms such as Regal Funds Management, Perpetual and others compete for institutional and high-net-worth capital outside the ASX wrapper. These are included to contextualise LSF’s positioning within the broader Australian alternative-equity universe.

### Market Share & Position
LSF is the largest dedicated long/short equity LIC on the ASX by a considerable margin. Its net tangible asset (NTA) backing per share was **A$4.0959 pre-tax** and **A$3.8034 post-tax** as at 1 May 2026 [Source: TipRanks / Title: L1 Long Short Fund Updates May 2026 Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-07] *. The listed vehicle’s implied NTA is roughly **A$2.5–2.7 billion** (inferred from NTA per share and an approximate share count derived from its trading economics profile and market capitalisation data) [Source: Trading Economics / Title: LSF:AU Assets / Date: 2025-12] *. By comparison, RF1 carried a market capitalisation of roughly **A$637 million** in mid-2025 [Source: Intelligent Investor / Title: Regal Investment Fund (ASX:RF1) / Date: 2025-07-22] *.  

The broader **L1 Long Short strategy** (including unlisted mandates and feeders) had approximately **A$4.5 billion** in assets under management as of mid-2025 [Source: Platinum Asset Management / Title: Scheme of Arrangement – Merger Implementation Agreement Explanatory Memorandum / Date: 2025-07-08] **, and industry data suggests the strategy had grown to roughly **A$5.9 billion** by December 2025 following a year of exceptional investment performance [Source: Trading Economics / Title: LSF:AU Assets / Date: 2025-12] *.  

### Relative Positioning
LSF’s **CY2025 portfolio return of 46.8%** (net of fees) dramatically outperformed the ASX 200 Accumulation Index, which was down roughly 1.0% over the same period [Source: L1 Capital / Title: Quarterly Report – December 2025 / Date: 2026-01-16] **. Over the five years to June 2025, the fund generated **21.0% p.a.** [Source: L1 Capital / Title: Quarterly Report – June 2025 / Date: 2025-07-17] **. This performance profile places LSF at the top of the peer heap on raw returns. RF1, by contrast, delivered an annualised return of **15.6% p.a.** since its June 2019 inception to May 2025 [Source: Regal Funds Management / Title: RF1 June 2025 Distribution Estimate / Date: 2025-06-30] **.  

Valuation discipline differs materially: LSF traded at a modest **premium to pre-tax NTA** in early May 2026 (share price around A$4.14–A$4.34 versus pre-tax NTA of ~A$4.10) [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: LSF.AX Historical Prices / Date: 2026-05-07] *; [Source: TipRanks / Title: L1 Long Short Fund Updates May 2026 Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-07] *. RF1, in contrast, has historically traded at a **discount to NAV** (its June 2025 distribution example implied a NAV of A$3.50 against a A$3.00 share price, a ~14% discount) [Source: Regal Funds Management / Title: RF1 June 2025 Distribution Estimate / Date: 2025-06-30] **. The premium suggests LSF currently enjoys scarcity value and strong retail demand for its track record.  

However, LSF’s **fee structure is elevated** relative to traditional LICs: a **1.44% p.a. management fee** and a **20.5% p.a. performance fee** with a high watermark [Source: L1 Capital / Title: 2025-02 LSF Monthly Report Monthly NTA / Date: 2025-02-28] **. This is competitive with hedge-fund peers but far above the ~0.1–1.0% typical of long-only LICs and ETFs.

## 2. Competitive Moat

### Moat Sources
1. **Investment Performance / Alpha Generation.** LSF’s most visible moat source is its track record. The fund’s 46.8% CY2025 return and multi-year double-digit annualised performance create a powerful marketing and distribution narrative. Research-house ratings amplify this: LSF holds a **“Recommended” rating from both Zenith and Lonsec** [Source: L1 Capital / Title: L1 Long Short Fund Limited / Date: 2026-04-20] **. In the Australian retail market, a “highly recommended” rating can drive a material FUM uplift [Source: ASIC / Title: Report 702 Competition in Funds Management / Date: 2021-09-24] *** (historical finding — verify current state).  
2. **Brand and Founder Reputation.** L1 Capital was founded in 2007 by Raphael Lamm and Mark Landau and has built a reputation for differentiated fundamental research [Source: L1 Capital / Title: 2025-02 LSF Monthly Report Monthly NTA / Date: 2025-02-28] **. The brand is a barrier to entry because retail investors and platforms gravitate towards managers with visible, long-tenured investment teams.  
3. **Scale Economies via L1 Group.** In October 2025, L1 Capital merged with Platinum Asset Management to form **L1 Group (ASX:L1G)**, with combined FUM of **A$16.7 billion** as at 31 October 2025 [Source: L1 Group / Title: Introduction to L1 Group – AGM Presentation / Date: 2025-11-18] **. The merged entity provides shared distribution, compliance and technology infrastructure, lowering marginal costs for LSF and broadening platform access.  
4. **High Watermark Fee Structure.** The 20.5% performance fee subject to a high watermark creates switching costs for investors who would forfeit the benefit of climbing back above the watermark if they redeem after a drawdown. It also aligns the manager’s incentive with absolute returns.  

### Moat Durability Assessment
- **Performance/Alpha: Eroding potential over the medium term.** Exceptional 2025 returns likely benefitted from a value/cyclical skew and idiosyncratic winners (e.g., Mineral Resources, Aurizon, BlueScope). Sustaining 20%+ annualised returns is improbable in a competitive long/short market. The moat is **stable to eroding over 2–5 years** as mean reversion pressures intensify.  
- **Brand/Ratings: Stable but contingent.** Zenith and Lonsec ratings are typically reviewed annually. A year or two of underperformance could trigger downgrades, eroding the distribution moat within 12–24 months. Currently stable.  
- **Scale: Strengthening.** The L1 Group merger completed in October 2025 expands distribution reach and operational leverage. This moat source is **strengthening** over the next 1–3 years as integration synergies (targeted at 25–30% of merged cost base) are realised [Source: Platinum Asset Management / Title: Scheme of Arrangement – Merger Implementation Agreement Explanatory Memorandum / Date: 2025-07-08] **.  
- **High Watermark: Structurally stable.** This is a contractual feature embedded in the fee structure; it persists unless the constitution is changed.  

## 3. Competitive Threats

- **Passive and Active ETF Competition.** Australian retail investors have migrated steadily toward lower-cost passive ETFs and, more recently, active ETFs. ASIC’s 2021 funds-management competition review found that passive growth has placed material fee pressure on active managers [Source: ASIC / Title: Report 702 Competition in Funds Management / Date: 2021-09-24] *** (historical — verify current state). Active ETFs offer intraday liquidity and often lower fees than LICs, threatening the listed-wrapper moat.  
- **Key-Person Risk.** LSF’s performance is tightly linked to L1 Capital’s founders and senior analysts. The departure of either founder or a significant portion of the investment team could rapidly degrade the brand and ratings moat. This is a **latent, high-severity threat**.  
- **Mean Reversion after an Exceptional Year.** CY2025’s 46.8% return sets a high bar. A return to mid-single-digit or negative years—common in long/short equity—could cause retail outflows and a collapse of the premium to NTA, pushing LSF toward the persistent discounts that plague many Australian LICs.  
- **Platform Gatekeeper Dynamics.** LSF’s distribution relies heavily on wealth platforms (BT Panorama, Netwealth, HUB24, Macquarie Wrap) [Source: L1 Capital / Title: 2025-02 LSF Monthly Report Monthly NTA / Date: 2025-02-28] **. These platforms increasingly use research-house ratings and fee-based screening to determine approved product lists. A fee downgrade or rating removal would materially constrain flows.  

## 4. Industry Growth & Demand Outlook (1–2 Year Horizon)

### Industry Growth Trajectory
The Australian funds-management industry is mature, with over 3,700 funds and more than 300 competitors [Source: ASIC / Title: Report 702 Competition in Funds Management / Date: 2021-09-24] *** (historical — verify current state). Industry-wide FUM growth is correlated with superannuation inflows and equity-market appreciation, but the **listed long/short equity niche** is a small subset. Demand for absolute-return strategies tends to be counter-cyclical: retail and institutional interest rises after market drawdowns or periods of high volatility when capital preservation becomes salient.  

### Demand Drivers & Headwinds
- **Driver:** Heightened equity-market volatility and macro uncertainty (U.S. tariff policy, RBA rate trajectory) may increase allocator appetite for long/short strategies that can reduce net exposure.  
- **Driver:** The L1 Group merger provides a larger distribution salesforce and institutional relationships, potentially channelling more flows into LSF over the next 18 months.  
- **Headwind:** Fee transparency and regulatory pressure on LIC fee structures may make LSF’s 1.44%/20.5% fee stack harder to justify if performance moderates.  
- **Headwind:** Retail broker and adviser platforms are increasingly favouring active ETFs over LICs because ETFs do not trade at persistent discounts/premiums and offer simpler tax reporting.  

### Industry Life Cycle & Structural Trends
Stable mature industry, no material life cycle commentary required. The Australian long/short equity sector is mature but evolving in *wrapper* form (shift from LICs to active ETFs). The structural trend to watch is wrapper disruption rather than industry decline.

## 5. Customer & Supplier Concentration

### Customer Concentration
LSF’s shareholder register is broad. **Insiders hold 21.35%** of shares (likely including L1 Capital principals and team) and **institutions hold 9.60%** of total shares (12.21% of the float) [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: LSF.XA Major Holders / Date: 2026-05-07] *. No single external holder appears to control a substantial block based on available substantial-shareholder disclosures [Source: MarketScreener / Title: L1 Long Short Fund Limited Major Shareholders / Date: 2026-05-07] *. The register is therefore **retail-dominated and unconcentrated**, which minimises single-investor redemption risk but exposes the fund to sentiment-driven retail flow volatility.  

### Supplier Dependencies
LSF is externally managed by **L1 Capital Pty Ltd**, now a subsidiary of **L1 Group Limited (ASX:L1G)** following the October 2025 merger [Source: L1 Group / Title: L1G Results for Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-25] **. This is the critical supplier relationship: all investment research, portfolio management and strategy are provided by L1 Capital. The fund also relies on prime brokerage, custody and fund-administration services, but these are generally replaceable and less material than the manager.  

A specific structural dependency arises from the **post-merger fee-split arrangement**. L1 Capital’s original shareholders retain **out-of-perimeter LSF performance fees** (returns above the first 3.5% p.a. hurdle) via Z Class shares, while the listed L1 Group receives only the first 3.5% slice of performance fees [Source: Platinum Asset Management / Title: Scheme of Arrangement – Explanatory Memorandum / Date: 2025-08-21] **. This creates a complex incentive structure whereby a portion of LSF’s performance-fee economics does not flow to L1 Group ordinary shareholders, potentially influencing capital-allocation decisions within the group.

## 6. Macroeconomic Sensitivities

### Standard Macro Variables
- **Interest Rates (High Relevance).** LSF runs a net-long book with target net exposure of **60–70%** over the cycle (allowing 30–100% net long) [Source: L1 Capital / Title: L1 Capital Long Short Fund – Monthly Class / Date: 2026-03-10] **. The fund uses leverage, meaning higher RBA cash rates directly increase borrowing costs and compress net returns. Conversely, rate cuts improve the cost of carry. Australian CPI was running at **4.0% y/y** in mid-2025, keeping the RBA in a hawkish posture [Source: L1 Capital / Title: Quarterly Report – June 2025 / Date: 2025-07-17] **. Any rate-cutting cycle in 2026 would be a tailwind.  
- **FX / AUD (High Relevance).** LSF holds material offshore exposures (e.g., U.S. cyclicals, U.K. banks, European infrastructure). **AUD depreciation** increases the AUD value of offshore assets and boosts reported returns; AUD appreciation creates a headwind.  
- **Equity Market Direction & Volatility (High Relevance).** Because LSF is net long, broad ASX 200 and global equity advances support returns; drawdowns hurt, though the short book and net-exposure flexibility provide partial mitigation. Volatility is a double-edged sword: it expands the opportunity set for short alpha but can also damage crowded long positions.  
- **Inflation (Moderate Relevance).** Affects portfolio holdings (e.g., James Hardie, Flight Centre) and RBA policy, but is largely an indirect portfolio-level exposure rather than a driver of fund-level revenue.  
- **Energy / Commodity Prices (Moderate Relevance).** LSF currently carries exposures to lithium, copper, gold and uranium [Source: L1 Capital / Title: Quarterly Report – December 2025 / Date: 2026-01-16] **. Commodity-price swings directly affect P&L.  
- **Labor Markets (Low Relevance).** Minimal direct impact on an externally managed LIC; indirect exposure via portfolio companies.  

### Company-Specific Macro Variables
- **Platform Distribution Economics.** LSF’s FUM growth is partly a function of adviser platform economics (e.g., Netwealth, HUB24, BT Panorama). Changes in platform administration fees or approved-product-list criteria can alter flow velocity.  
- **Research-House Rating Cycle.** The timing of Zenith/Lonsec annual reviews acts as a quasi-macro variable for retail flows.  

## 7. Regulatory Environment

LSF operates under **ASIC oversight** as an Australian-listed investment company and is subject to the Corporations Act, ASX Listing Rules and continuous disclosure obligations. Key regulatory dimensions include:

- **Short-Selling Disclosure.** The fund’s short positions are subject to ASIC’s short-selling reporting regime, including the “short sale” flagging and daily disclosure thresholds for substantial short interests.  
- **NTA Reporting.** LSF reports unaudited NTA per share monthly, a standard LIC practice that provides transparency but also creates market volatility around release dates [Source: TipRanks / Title: L1 Long Short Fund Updates May 2026 Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-07] *.  
- **Performance Fee Disclosure & LIC Tax Treatment.** The high performance-fee structure (20.5%) is disclosed in the fund’s constitution and product disclosure. Any regulatory push to cap or further disclose performance fees in LICs—similar to scrutiny in the superannuation sector—could affect competitive positioning.  
- **Merger Overhang (Resolved).** The L1 Capital–Platinum merger was completed in **October 2025** [Source: L1 Group / Title: L1G Results for Half-Year Ended 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-25] **. ACCC clearance was obtained and the merged L1 Group is now the responsible entity’s ultimate parent. No active regulatory overhang remains, though the unusual Z Class share fee-split structure is a bespoke contractual arrangement rather than a regulatory issue.  

## 8. Technology & Structural Disruption Risks

### Identified Disruption Threats
1. **AI-Driven Quantitative Strategies.** Machine-learning and alternative-data-driven quant funds are increasingly competing for the same fundamental alpha in equity long/short. Over a 2–5 year horizon, systematic strategies could erode the mispricing opportunities that LSF’s fundamental research relies upon, particularly in mid-cap and event-driven situations.  
2. **Active ETF Disintermediation of the LIC Wrapper.** Active ETFs offer intraday liquidity, no discount/premium dynamics and typically lower fee structures. If major Australian managers launch lower-cost long/short active ETFs, the LIC structure—with its fixed capital, premium/discount volatility and higher fee stack—could become structurally disadvantaged for retail distribution.  
3. **Fee Compression from Passive and Semi-Active Products.** The global trend toward 10–50 basis-point equity products places long-term pressure on LSF’s 1.44% management fee. While performance fees can offset this, a sustained period of benchmark-relative underperformance would make the fee stack untenable for many retail advisers.  

### Timeline & Probability Assessment
- **AI/Quant Alpha Erosion:** Medium-term (2–5 years), **moderate probability**. Would require widespread adoption of alternative data and factor crowding in the Australian small/mid-cap segment where LSF hunts.  
- **Active ETF Wrapper Shift:** Medium-term (2–5 years), **moderate-to-high probability**. Regulatory and exchange infrastructure for active ETFs is already in place in Australia; adoption is growing.  
- **Fee Compression:** Ongoing (0–5+ years), **high probability** for the industry, but LSF may be partially insulated near-term by its performance premium.  

### Company Response & Adaptation
- **Scale and Diversification via L1 Group Merger.** The merged A$16.7bn group provides resources to invest in quantitative research tools and data analytics alongside fundamental research.  
- **Extension Strategies.** L1 Group is already launching adjacent strategies (e.g., L1 Global Long Short) to capture flows without altering LSF’s core mandate [Source: L1 Group / Title: Introduction to L1 Group – AGM Presentation / Date: 2025-11-18] **.  
- **Platform Distribution Maintenance.** LSF maintains presence on all major Australian platforms (BT Panorama, Netwealth, HUB24, Macquarie Wrap, etc.) and holds Recommended ratings from Zenith and Lonsec [Source: L1 Capital / Title: L1 Long Short Fund Limited / Date: 2026-04-20] **.  

## 9. Bottom-Line Competitive & Macro Assessment

### Competitive Position Characterization
LSF is the **dominant ASX-listed long/short equity vehicle**, distinguished by superior recent performance, a premium-to-NTA valuation, strong research-house ratings and brand recognition. It sits within a maturing but still-growing alternative-asset niche in Australian wealth management. However, its competitive position is **performance-dependent**: the premium valuation and retail flow momentum are artifacts of an exceptional 2025 return year that will be difficult to repeat.

### Key Competitive & Macro Drivers (confidence weighted)
1. **Sustained investment performance relative to ASX 200 and peer funds** — **High confidence**. This is the single largest driver of flows, ratings and premium/discount dynamics over the next 12–24 months.  
2. **RBA interest-rate trajectory and cost of leverage** — **High confidence**. Directly affects net returns given the fund’s leveraged, net-long posture.  
3. **L1 Group merger integration and distribution leverage** — **Medium confidence**. Synergies are targeted but execution risk remains in cross-selling Platinum’s global equity capability alongside L1’s alternative suite.  
4. **AUD/USD exchange-rate movements** — **Medium confidence**. Material offshore portfolio exposure makes this a persistent P&L driver.  
5. **Retail platform and research-house rating maintenance** — **Medium confidence**. Zenith/Lonsec renewals and platform approved-product-list status are gatekeepers for retail flows.  

### Key Competitive & Macro Risks (confidence weighted)
1. **Performance mean reversion after CY2025’s 46.8% return** — **High confidence**. A return to mid-single-digit years would likely collapse the premium to NTA and trigger retail outflows.  
2. **Key-person departure at L1 Capital** — **Medium confidence**. The manager brand is founder-dependent; loss of either Lamm or Landau would likely trigger a ratings review and flow exodus.  
3. **Active ETF wrapper competition and LIC fee compression** — **Medium confidence**. A structural headwind that threatens the entire LIC sector, particularly high-fee active vehicles.  
4. **RBA rate hikes or prolonged elevated rates** — **Medium confidence**. Would increase borrowing costs and weigh on portfolio valuations simultaneously.  

### Catalysts to Watch
- **Monthly NTA releases** (early each month): Next report due early June 2026; watch for premium/discount trend.  
- **L1 Group FY26 results** (expected August 2026): Will update on Platinum FUM stabilisation, LSF strategy FUM and integration cost synergies.  
- **RBA Board meetings** (next typically first Tuesday of each month): Cash-rate decisions affect leverage costs and equity-market sentiment.  
- **Zenith/Lonsec annual research rating reviews** (timing not publicly fixed, typically mid-calendar year): A downgrade would be a material negative catalyst.  
- **June 2026 quarterly portfolio update** (expected July 2026): Will reveal net exposure positioning and any performance fee crystallisation events.  

## 10. Data Gaps & Limitations

- **Exact listed-vehicle AUM/NTA in dollar terms.** No precise aggregate NTA figure for the listed LSF vehicle was found; the A$5.93 billion figure from Trading Economics appears to refer to the broader L1 Long Short strategy (including unlisted mandates) rather than the LIC alone. Search scope: ASX announcements, L1 Capital monthly reports, TipRanks NTA updates. Analytical impact: market-share estimates are inferred from NTA per share and approximated share count.  
- **RF1’s CY2025 full-year return.** Only an annualised since-inception figure to May 2025 was located. Search scope: Regal Funds Management ASX announcements, RF1 quarterly reports. Analytical impact: direct calendar-year peer comparison is incomplete.  
- **LSF’s precise net exposure as at May 2026.** Only strategic target ranges (30–100% net long, typically 60–70%) were found; the most recent actual net long figure was from the December 2025 quarterly commentary noting a reduction toward long-term average. Search scope: LSF monthly and quarterly ASX announcements. Analytical impact: current leverage and market-sensitivity estimates are directional rather than precise.  
- **Top 20 shareholder register post-merger.** Current substantial-holder data is sparse and may not reflect post-merger ownership changes. Search scope: ASX substantial shareholder notices, LSF annual report 2025. Analytical impact: customer-concentration assessment relies on aggregate institutional/retail breakdown from Yahoo Finance rather than named holders.  
- **Stale industry-wide data on fund flows.** The most authoritative structural analysis of Australian funds-management competition is ASIC Report 702 (September 2021). Search scope: ASIC website, Morningstar/Iress flow data. Analytical impact: industry competitive dynamics are characterised using a four-year-old regulatory study; more recent flow data would sharpen the passive-versus-active assessment.
